## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
*What is Project-Based Learning (PBL) in the context of Digital Electronics and Logic System Design? List the five phases of the KTU-prescribed PBL lifecycle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Project-Based Learning (PBL) in Digital Electronics is a **student-centered pedagogical approach** where learners design, simulate, implement, and verify a complete digital hardware artifact (such as an ALU, counter system, or controller) starting from a functional specification.

The **five phases** of the KTU-prescribed PBL lifecycle are:

1. **Problem Identification & Specification** — Define inputs, outputs, and constraints.
2. **Conceptualization & Block-Level Design** — Decompose into functional sub-blocks.
3. **Detailed Design (Schematic / HDL)** — Convert blocks into gate-level or HDL modules.
4. **Simulation & Functional Verification** — Test using testbenches or manual stimulus.
5. **Implementation, Testing & Documentation** — Realize on hardware and submit report.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the five phases.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State any three advantages of implementing a digital design on an FPGA rather than using discrete 74-series TTL ICs on a breadboard.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Reconfigurability** — An FPGA can be reprogrammed无数次 via HDL code changes, while a breadboard prototype must be rewired for every modification. *(1 mark)*
2. **Higher Integration & Density** — A single FPGA can realize thousands of gates, flip-flops, and even embedded multipliers, replacing dozens of ICs and reducing wiring complexity. *(1 mark)*
3. **Built-in Debugging & I/O** — Modern FPGA boards (e.g., Xilinx Spartan-7, Intel Cyclone) include switches, LEDs, 7-segment displays, and USB programmers, enabling rapid prototyping and in-system verification. *(1 mark)*

*(Alternative 3rd advantage: Lower power per gate, or easier simulation-to-hardware flow.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-end questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Design a **4-bit synchronous up-counter** using **JK flip-flops**. Derive the excitation table, the K-map simplifications for each flip-flop input, and draw the logic circuit. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain how this counter can be used as a **frequency divider** with a division ratio of **10** (i.e., a Mod-10 / BCD counter). Show the reset logic. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — State Table for 4-bit Up-Counter:**

The counter has 16 states: $0000 \to 0001 \to 0010 \to \dots \to 1111 \to 0000$.

The flip-flop outputs are $Q_3 Q_2 Q_1 Q_0$ (with $Q_0$ as LSB).

**Step 2 — JK Excitation Table:**

For a JK flip-flop, the required inputs $(J, K)$ to achieve a transition $Q \to Q_{next}$ are:

| $Q$ | $Q_{next}$ | $J$ | $K$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | X |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | X |
| 1 | 0 | X | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | X | 0 |

**Step 3 — Excitation Table for All Flip-Flops:**

| Present State $Q_3 Q_2 Q_1 Q_0$ | Next State $Q_3^+ Q_2^+ Q_1^+ Q_0^+$ | $J_3 K_3$ | $J_2 K_2$ | $J_1 K_1$ | $J_0 K_0$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0000 | 0001 | 0X | 0X | 0X | 1X |
| 0001 | 0010 | 0X | 0X | 1X | X1 |
| 0010 | 0011 | 0X | 0X | X0 | 1X |
| 0011 | 0100 | 0X | 1X | X1 | X1 |
| 0100 | 0101 | 0X | X0 | 0X | 1X |
| 0101 | 0110 | 0X | X0 | 1X | X1 |
| 0110 | 0111 | 0X | X0 | X0 | 1X |
| 0111 | 1000 | 1X | X1 | X1 | X1 |
| 1000 | 1001 | X0 | 0X | 0X | 1X |
| 1001 | 1010 | X0 | 0X | 1X | X1 |
| 1010 | 1011 | X0 | 0X | X0 | 1X |
| 1011 | 1100 | X0 | 1X | X1 | X1 |
| 1100 | 1101 | X0 | X0 | 0X | 1X |
| 1101 | 1110 | X0 | X0 | 1X | X1 |
| 1110 | 1111 | X0 | X0 | X0 | 1X |
| 1111 | 0000 | X1 | X1 | X1 | X1 |

**Step 4 — K-Map Simplifications:**

For **FF0** (LSB): $Q_0$ toggles every clock, so:

$$
J_0 = K_0 = 1
$$

For **FF1**: Toggles when $Q_0 = 1$:

$$
J_1 = K_1 = Q_0
$$

For **FF2**: Toggles when $Q_0 = Q_1 = 1$:

$$
J_2 = K_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1
$$

For **FF3** (MSB): Toggles when $Q_0 = Q_1 = Q_2 = 1$:

$$
J_3 = K_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2
$$

**Step 5 — Logic Circuit:**

Four JK flip-flops cascaded, with the J and K inputs of each stage driven by the AND of all lower-order Q outputs.

**Step 6 — Verification:**

Initial state: $0000$. After 1 clock: $0001$. After 2 clocks: $0010$. ... After 16 clocks: back to $0000$. ✓

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

To convert the **Mod-16 counter** into a **Mod-10 (BCD) counter**, we must reset the counter when the count reaches $1010_2 = 10_{10}$.

**Step 1 — Detect Count = 10:**

The binary representation of 10 is $Q_3 Q_2 Q_1 Q_0 = 1010$. We need a combinational circuit that detects this state:

$$
\text{Reset} = Q_3 \cdot \overline{Q_2} \cdot Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Asynchronous Reset:**

Feed this Reset signal to the **CLR'** (active-low asynchronous clear) input of all four JK flip-flops.

**Step 3 — Count Sequence:**

$0000 \to 0001 \to 0010 \to 0011 \to 0100 \to 0101 \to 0110 \to 0111 \to 1000 \to 1001 \to \text{RESET} \to 0000$

The count goes from 0 to 9 (10 states) and wraps around. ✓

**Step 4 — Frequency Division:**

If the input clock frequency is $f_{in}$, the output taken from $Q_3$ (after dividing by 10) is:

$$
f_{out} = \frac{f_{in}}{10}
$$

For example, with $f_{in} = 1 \text{ MHz}$:

$$
f_{out} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{10} = 100{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ kHz}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the architecture of a **4-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** that supports the operations: **ADD, SUB, AND, OR**. Describe the role of the **mode select inputs** and the **carry/borrow flag**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the ADD operation, derive the **sum and carry equations** of a 1-bit full adder using a **Karnaugh Map**. Then, compute the **worst-case propagation delay** if the ALU is implemented using a 4-bit ripple carry adder with $t_{pd,FA} = 12 \text{ ns}$ and a 4-to-1 output multiplexer with $t_{pd,MUX} = 6 \text{ ns}$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Architecture (Block Diagram Description):**

The 4-bit ALU consists of the following interconnected blocks (as derived in Section 3):

1. **4-bit Ripple Carry Adder (RCA):** Built from four 1-bit Full Adders. Performs A + B (ADD mode) or A + B' + 1 (SUB mode).
2. **Inverter Array:** Inverts B bit-by-bit when SUB is selected.
3. **AND Array & OR Array:** Bit-wise logic operations.
4. **4-to-1 Multiplexer (per bit):** Selects between ADD, SUB, AND, OR outputs.
5. **Mode Select Decoder:** Interprets the M[1:0] inputs and routes them to the appropriate sub-blocks.

**Mode Select Inputs:**

| M1 | M0 | Operation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | ADD | Adder output routed to MUX; B is not inverted; Cin = 0. |
| 0 | 1 | SUB | Adder output routed to MUX; B is inverted; Cin = 1. |
| 1 | 0 | AND | AND array output routed to MUX. |
| 1 | 1 | OR | OR array output routed to MUX. |

**Carry/Borrow Flag:**

- For **ADD**: $C_{flag} = C_{out}$ of the ripple carry adder.
- For **SUB**: $C_{flag} = \overline{C_{out}}$ of the adder (since in 2's complement subtraction, the complement of the final carry indicates the borrow).
- For **AND** and **OR**: $C_{flag} = 0$ (no carry generated).

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1 — K-Map for Sum:**

(As derived in Section 3.3)

| Sum K-map cells: | 00 | 01 | 11 | 10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A\cdot B = 00$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $A\cdot B = 01$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $A\cdot B = 11$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $A\cdot B = 10$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

This is a checkerboard pattern → Sum is the **3-input XOR**:

$$
S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}
$$

**Step 2 — K-Map for Carry:**

| Cout K-map: | $C_{in}=0$ | $C_{in}=1$ |
|---|---|---|
| $A\cdot B = 00$ | 0 | 0 |
| $A\cdot B = 01$ | 0 | 1 |
| $A\cdot B = 11$ | 1 | 1 |
| $A\cdot B = 10$ | 0 | 1 |

Simplified:

$$
C_{out} = A \cdot B + (A \oplus B) \cdot C_{in}
$$

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Propagation Delay Calculation:**

The longest path in the ALU is:
**Input → 4 Full Adders (carry chain) → Multiplexer → Output**

$$
t_{pd,total} = 4 \cdot t_{pd,FA} + t_{pd,MUX}
$$

Substituting:

$$
t_{pd,total} = 4 \times 12 \text{ ns} + 6 \text{ ns} = 48 \text{ ns} + 6 \text{ ns} = 54 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Operating Frequency:**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{pd,total}} = \frac{1}{54 \text{ ns}} \approx 18.52 \text{ MHz}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget that the **ripple carry path** is the critical path, not the sum-bit path. Each full adder's carry must propagate through all four stages **sequentially**, even though the sum bits are computed in parallel. Also, do NOT confuse the MUX delay as being on the input side — the MUX is on the **output side** of all four sub-blocks. Marks are commonly lost here.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 PBL**

- **PBL Definition:** Student-centered, project-driven learning culminating in a working digital artifact. *(KTU 2024 emphasis)*
- **Five Phases:** Specification → Block Design → Detailed Design → Simulation → Implementation & Documentation.
- **Typical Deliverables:** Working prototype (FPGA / breadboard), technical report, viva-voce, demonstration.
- **4-bit ALU** is the most commonly asked PBL exam problem — master its block diagram, adder/subtractor equations, and MUX-based selection logic.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
   - Full Adder Sum: $S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$
   - Full Adder Carry: $C_{out} = A \cdot B + (A \oplus B) \cdot C_{in}$
   - Subtractor identity: $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$
   - Counter toggle input: $J_i = K_i = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdots Q_{i-1}$
   - Worst-case delay (RCA + MUX): $t_{pd} = n \cdot t_{pd,FA} + t_{pd,MUX}$
- **FPGA Advantages:** Reconfigurability, high integration, built-in debug features, in-system reprogramming.
- **Evaluation Rubric:** Innovation (10%) + Design Correctness (30%) + Implementation (30%) + Documentation (15%) + Viva (15%).
- **Common Viva Questions:**
   - "Why use a ripple carry adder instead of a carry look-ahead adder?" → *Trade-off: simpler design, slower speed.*
   - "What is the role of a testbench?" → *To apply stimulus and verify HDL model behavior before hardware implementation.*
   - "How does 2's complement enable subtraction using an adder?" → *Inversion + carry-in logic.*
- **Tool Familiarity:** Be ready to discuss **Xilinx Vivado**, **Intel Quartus**, **ModelSim**, or **TinkerCAD** for circuit simulation.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Never present a project that is purely software simulation (no hardware). The KTU panel expects **at least a working breadboard or FPGA demonstration**.
- **Report Structure:** Title page → Abstract → Introduction → Block Diagram → Schematic/HDL → Simulation Results → Hardware Photos → Conclusion → References.
- **Module 4 Coverage:** PBL projects typically integrate sequential logic (counters, registers), memory (RAM/ROM), and HDL — ensure your project touches at least **two** of these areas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering exam questions on PBL, always frame your answer around the **engineering design process** (specify → design → simulate → build → test). This terminology is what the KTU paper evaluators are trained to look for and award full marks for.
